Synthetic Canadians: Comparing Synthetic Survey Data Generators

2021 Democracy Checkup — Political Attitudes Validation Study

Author

Synthetic Opinion Lab

Published

March 13, 2026

Overview

This report validates three synthetic data generation approaches against the 2021 Democracy Checkup (DC21), a national survey of Canadian political attitudes (n = 7,576).

The 21 variables span five substantive domains: government satisfaction, institutional confidence, political efficacy, immigration attitudes, social conservatism, and economic positions, plus party identification and vote choice.

Three generators are compared:

Generator N synthetic Overall quality score
LLM (Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct) 100 0.803
IRT (Graded Response Model) 1,000 0.625
Agent (Opinion dynamics) 500 0.548

Mean Responses by Variable

Response Distributions

Distribution of responses (%) for each variable. First 12 shown; remaining 9 below.

Standard Deviations

Higher SD indicates the generator produces meaningful variance across respondents. Real SD is ~0.8–1.2 for most items.

Correlation Structure

How well does each generator preserve the inter-item correlation matrix of the real data?

Frobenius distance from real correlation matrix:
  IRT                 : Frobenius = 4.60, r(lower tri) = 0.397
  LLM                 : Frobenius = 5.17, r(lower tri) = 0.676
  Agent               : Frobenius = 15.94, r(lower tri) = 0.177

K-Means Clustering (k=3)

K-means with k=3 on the 19 attitude items (excluding party_id and vote_choice). Cluster centres reveal opinion profiles.

Cluster interpretation (ordered low → high average response):

Real (n=7,576)

  • Cluster 1 (39%): avg=2.23 — highest: Imm: Take Jobs (2.9), Econ: Private Sector Jobs (2.8)
  • Cluster 2 (28%): avg=2.39 — highest: Fed Gov Satisfaction (3.4), Confidence: Fed Gov (3.3)
  • Cluster 3 (33%): avg=2.62 — highest: Imm: Take Jobs (3.8), Imm: Canada First (3.6)

IRT (n=1,000)

  • Cluster 1 (37%): avg=2.32 — highest: Efficacy: Govt Cares (3.3), Confidence: Fed Gov (2.9)
  • Cluster 2 (28%): avg=2.62 — highest: Confidence: Fed Gov (3.6), Confidence: Courts (3.6)
  • Cluster 3 (35%): avg=2.81 — highest: Econ: Reduce Inequality (3.9), Imm: Assimilation (3.7)

LLM (n=100)

  • Cluster 1 (37%): avg=2.06 — highest: Imm: Take Jobs (3.1), Imm: Assimilation (3.0)
  • Cluster 2 (31%): avg=2.18 — highest: Confidence: Media (3.0), Fed Gov Satisfaction (3.0)
  • Cluster 3 (32%): avg=2.47 — highest: Imm: Assimilation (3.7), Imm: Take Jobs (3.4)

Agent (n=500)

  • Cluster 1 (37%): avg=1.08 — highest: Fed Gov Satisfaction (1.3), Efficacy: Govt Cares (1.3)
  • Cluster 2 (26%): avg=2.01 — highest: Econ: Reduce Inequality (2.5), Fed Gov Satisfaction (2.0)
  • Cluster 3 (37%): avg=2.91 — highest: Econ: Reduce Inequality (3.9), Confidence: Courts (3.0)

Regression Replication: Vote Choice Predictors

Can we replicate a logistic regression predicting Conservative vs. Liberal vote choice from attitude items?

Summary

Dimension Real IRT LLM Agent
N respondents 7,576 1,000 100 500
Overall quality score — 0.625 0.803 0.548
Valid response range ✅ ✅ (fixed) ✅ ✅
Mean-level accuracy — Partial Good Partial
Variance accuracy — Good Moderate Moderate
Correlation structure — Moderate Best Weak

LLM (Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct-Turbo) achieves the highest overall fidelity score (0.803). The persona narratives provide sufficient context about political orientations for the model to produce plausible response distributions. Correlation structure is best preserved.

IRT (score 0.625) generates valid values in the correct 1–8 range (after fixing 0-based indexing). The GRM parameters are randomly generated rather than calibrated from real data, which limits mean-level accuracy. Calibrating parameters from the real data would substantially improve performance.

Agent simulation (score 0.548) converges quickly (11 of 25 timesteps) to a moderate uniform intensity level. The item-specific trait mappings partially differentiate items, but the social influence dynamics smooth out variance. Longer simulation or stronger initial opinion variance could improve this.
